title: Was This Fossil Creature the First Right-Hander Ever?
gist: A Scientific Reports study of the ~550-million-year-old Ediacaran organism Spriggina floundersi finds it consistently favored bending to its right side, potentially the earliest known evidence of biological handedness.

insights.claim: The Ediacaran Period (~635 to 535 million years ago) saw life transition from single cells to macroscopic multicellular organisms that first developed rudimentary sensory organs and began moving around.
insights.speaker: Jake Currie
insights.so_what: Context for when key animal traits first appeared.

insights.claim: A Scientific Reports study found roughly twice as many Spriggina floundersi fossils preserved curled to the left, which, because fossils are mirror images, means the living animals preferred bending right, possibly the first known handedness.
insights.speaker: Jake Currie
insights.so_what: Pushes the origin of lateralized behavior back half a billion years.

insights.claim: Spriggina floundersi, the official fossil of South Australia, resembles a trilobite but its classification remains disputed, ranging from worms to an extinct fern-like animal group.
insights.speaker: Jake Currie
insights.so_what: Underlines unresolved placement of early Ediacaran fauna.
